title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Nudge: Architecture of Choice
content.subtitle: How subtle changes in environment can influence human behavior without restricting freedom.
content.text: For decades, traditional economic models were built on the image of 'Homo Economicus'—a perfectly rational being who consistently makes optimal decisions to maximize their own well-being. However, real-world observation tells a different story. Humans are impulsive, prone to procrastination, and easily swayed by the way information is presented. [31] Recognizing these flaws, a new field known as behavioral economics has emerged, suggesting that we can be 'nudged' toward better choices without the need for bans or financial penalties. [/31] || A nudge is essentially any aspect of the choice architecture that alters people's behavior in a predictable way without forbidding any options. A classic example is the placement of fruit at eye level in a school cafeteria while moving junk food to harder-to-reach shelves. [32] This doesn't stop a student from buying a candy bar, but it leverages our natural tendency to pick the most convenient option, resulting in a significant increase in healthy eating habits. [/32] It is about designing for the brain we actually have, rather than the one we think we have. || One of the most powerful tools in the nudger's toolkit is the 'default option.' Humans have a powerful bias toward the status quo; we tend to go with the flow of what has already been decided for us. [33] This is most evident in organ donation rates: countries where citizens must 'opt out' have nearly 100% participation, while those that require an 'opt in' struggle with much lower numbers, despite similar levels of public support. [/33] The default setting does the heavy lifting for our lazy, overloaded minds. || [34] Critics, however, raise concerns about the ethics of 'libertarian paternalism.' They argue that while nudges are subtle, they can still be manipulative, steering people toward the government's or a corporation's idea of what is 'good.' [/34] There is a fine line between a helpful nudge and a deceptive push. If the choice architect has hidden motives, the transparency of the nudge is compromised, and the individual's autonomy is subtly eroded. || Furthermore, there is the question of 'sludge.' While a nudge helps you make a better choice, 'sludge' refers to intentional frictions designed to make a beneficial action more difficult. [35] Think of the arduous, multi-step process required to cancel a subscription or the complex paperwork needed to access social benefits. [/35] Sludge exploits our cognitive weaknesses to keep us trapped in behaviors that benefit the provider rather than the user. It is the dark mirror of behavioral design. || [36] As we move further into the digital age, the power of nudging is amplified by data. [/36] Algorithms now know our weaknesses better than we do, presenting us with personalized nudges in real-time. Whether this leads to a world of optimized health and productivity or one of total psychological manipulation depends on our ability to demand ethical standards from those who design our digital environments. The architecture is already there; the question is who it is building for.

questions.question: What does the author identify as the origin of behavioral economics?
options: A) The discovery that humans are more rational than previously thought.
B) A desire to replace all financial penalties with subtle suggestions.
C) The realization that human decision-making is fundamentally imperfect.
D) A need for governments to ban unhealthy products more effectively.
questions.explanation: The text states that behavioral economics emerged from 'recognizing these flaws' (impulsiveness, procrastination) in the rational model.

questions.question: The cafeteria example in the second paragraph is used to demonstrate that...
options: A) healthy food must be made cheaper to attract students.
B) people can be influenced by how easy it is to access an option.
C) banning junk food is the only way to improve children's diets.
D) students are unaware of the nutritional value of their choices.
questions.explanation: The text says it 'leverages our natural tendency to pick the most convenient option' without forbidding any other choices.

questions.question: What point is made about organ donation in the third paragraph?
options: A) Most people are morally opposed to being organ donors.
B) Participation is driven more by the pre-selected choice than by personal belief.
C) 'Opt-in' systems are more ethical than 'opt-out' systems.
D) Digital communication has made 'opting out' much easier than before.
questions.explanation: The text notes that countries with 'opt out' (default) have nearly 100% participation despite 'similar levels of public support' in other countries, showing the power of the status quo.

questions.question: What is the 'fine line' mentioned by the author in the fourth paragraph?
options: A) The difference between a government nudge and a corporate one.
B) The boundary between providing helpful guidance and exercising control.
C) The cost of implementing a nudge versus the benefit it provides.
D) The speed at which a nudge can change a person's long-term habits.
questions.explanation: The author contrasts a 'helpful nudge' with a 'deceptive push' that erodes autonomy and compromises transparency.

questions.question: Which of the following would the author classify as 'sludge'?
options: A) A pop-up reminder to drink water every hour.
B) A simplified tax form that takes only five minutes to complete.
C) A website that makes it extremely confusing to delete an account.
D) A discount offered to customers who pay their bills on time.
questions.explanation: The text defines sludge as 'intentional frictions' like an 'arduous, multi-step process' to cancel a subscription.

questions.question: In the final paragraph, the author expresses concern that digital nudging...
options: A) is less effective than physical nudging because of data privacy.
B) will lead to a decrease in global productivity.
C) could be used to exploit individual psychological vulnerabilities.
D) is being ignored by the people who design ethical standards.
questions.explanation: The text states that algorithms 'know our weaknesses' and this could lead to 'total psychological manipulation' if ethical standards aren't met.
